title: Why Dense Representations Win
concept: Dense vectors capture relationships in low-dimensional space, solving the memory and semantic limits of one-hot vectors. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Word2Vec.'

exercises.prompt: What are the two main limitations of one-hot vectors that dense representations solve?

options.text: They make vectors much larger and harder to store.
High memory usage and lack of word relationships.

exercises.explain: One-hot encoding creates huge, sparse vectors where words have no mathematical relationship to each other. Dense vectors solve this by compressing words into a smaller, shared space where relationships can be measured.

cards.title: The Vector Space Problem
cards.body: Imagine trying to build a map of the world, but every single city is placed in a completely different dimension so that none of them connect. That is how one-hot encoding works! It uses massive amounts of memory and doesn't know that 'Rome' and 'Paris' are both European cities. Dense representations fix this by putting everything on a compact, meaningful map.

cards.title: What are Dense Representations?
cards.body: Dense representations compress word meanings into small, compact lists of decimal numbers. Instead of thousands of zeros, every position in a dense vector contains a meaningful value, allowing the computer to represent rich relationships in a very small amount of memory.

cards.title: The Cozy Library
cards.body: Imagine a library. A one-hot representation is like giving every single book its own giant room. The library becomes endlessly massive and hard to walk through. A dense representation is like placing books on cozy shelves based on their genre. Books about space sit next to each other, saving space while keeping related topics together.

cards.title: Spot the Similarity
cards.body: Instead of representing 'cat' and 'dog' as isolated points in a 50,000-dimensional space, a dense vector might use only 3 dimensions: [cuteness, fluffiness, size]. Both words will have similar, close numbers because they share these traits, unlike 'toaster' which would look completely different.

cards.title: Is Smaller Worse?
cards.body: You might think that because dense vectors are smaller (e.g., 300 numbers instead of 50,000), they contain less information. In fact, they contain far MORE useful information! By using decimals instead of just 0 and 1, they can represent subtle shades of meaning and relationships.

cards.title: One-Hot vs. Dense Representation
cards.body: See how the layout changes when we switch from one-hot to dense representations.

left.text: Massive, mostly zeros, cannot show that 'king' and 'queen' are related.

right.text: Compact, filled with decimals, groups related words close together.

exercises.prompt: How do dense vectors handle a growing vocabulary compared to one-hot vectors?

options.text: They double in size every time a new word is added.
They only work for numbers, not words.
They keep a fixed, small size even as the vocabulary grows.
They require billions of dimensions for simple sentences.

exercises.explain: One-hot vectors grow larger with every new word added to the vocabulary. Dense vectors stay the same size (e.g., 300 dimensions) no matter how many words you have.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Dense vectors represent words as points in a continuous space, using decimal values.
exercises.explain: Dense vectors represent words using active decimal numbers (like 0.25, -0.8) rather than giant lists of zeros and a single one.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this memory-saving format.
exercises.sentence: We use a ________ representation to pack word meanings into a small number of dimensions.
exercises.explain: Dense vectors use a small, fixed size to store detailed numerical coordinates, saving computer memory while retaining semantic meaning.

exercises.prompt: Which of these representations shows that two words have similar meanings by keeping their numbers close?

options.text: Vector A: [0.8, -0.4, 0.1] and Vector B: [0.7, -0.3, 0.2] (Dense similarity)

exercises.explain: AI models use dense vectors because they represent words as coordinates where similar words are placed close together. One-hot vectors cannot show similarity.

exercises.prompt: Why are dense representations preferred over one-hot representations in modern AI?

options.text: They make vectors much longer and harder for computers to read.
They save memory and capture actual meaning between words.
They replace words with giant tables of zeros.
They prevent machines from finding similarities.

exercises.explain: Dense representations pack rich meaning into a small, fixed number of dimensions, which solves both the memory explosion and the lack of relationship data in one-hot vectors. Next up: Word2Vec.
